learn.title: Small Wind Turbines for Homes
learn.intro: Small wind turbines let households and villages generate clean power locally, reducing reliance on diesel generators and grid outages. Understanding their operation helps communities tap into abundant wind resources and accelerate the transition to renewable energy in remote areas.
learn.c1_text: Wind pushes the turbine blades, turning a low‑speed shaft like a pinwheel. A typical 5 kW home turbine captures wind at 12 m/s, producing enough electricity to run 10–12 average Indian homes, similar to a garden fan generating motion.
learn.c2_text: The slow rotor motion is increased by a gearbox to spin a generator at 1,500 rpm, converting mechanical energy into electricity. Direct‑drive models skip the gearbox, using larger magnets for smoother operation and lower maintenance.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of New & Renewable Energy targets 5 GW of small wind capacity by 2030, focusing on states like Gujarat and Tamil Nadu where community wind farms already power schools and clinics.
learn.stat2: Average capacity factor for small turbines: 25 %
learn.stat3: Typical home turbine rotor diameter: 3–5 m
learn.stat4: Wind speed needed for 5 kW output: ~12 m/s

learn.title: Solar PV and the Photoelectric Effect
learn.intro: Photovoltaic panels turn sunlight directly into electricity, a cornerstone of the global clean‑energy shift. Grasping the photoelectric effect reveals how everyday rooftops become power plants, cutting emissions and electricity bills.
learn.c1_text: When photons strike a silicon cell, they knock electrons loose, creating a flow of charge. One square meter of panel receives about 1,000 W of sunlight, enough to free millions of electrons each second—like rain splashing on a pond and creating ripples.
learn.c2_text: The cell’s built‑in electric field pushes freed electrons toward the front contact, generating a voltage of ~0.6 V per cell. Connecting 60 cells in series yields ~36 V, enough to power household appliances when combined into a module.
learn.c3_text: India’s Solar Mission aims for 300 GW of PV by 2030. Large‑scale farms in Rajasthan now supply power to over 2 million homes, while rooftop installations grow 30 % annually, supported by net‑metering policies.
learn.stat3: Average solar irradiance in India: 5.5 kWh/m²/day
learn.stat4: Lifetime energy yield: ~1,500 kWh per kW installed

learn.title: Flow Batteries for Grid Storage
learn.intro: Flow batteries store energy in liquid electrolytes, offering long‑duration storage for renewable grids. They can balance supply and demand, making wind and solar more reliable for large‑scale power systems.
learn.c1_text: Energy is stored in two external tanks—one with vanadium ions, the other with electrolyte solution. Pumping them through a cell stack creates electricity, similar to water flowing through a hydro turbine, but with chemicals instead of water.
learn.c2_text: Power (MW) depends on the size of the cell stack, while energy (MWh) scales with tank volume. A 10 MW/40 MWh vanadium system can be expanded by adding more tanks without changing the stack, offering flexible sizing.
learn.c3_text: The Indian Renewable Energy Development Agency funded a 5 MW/20 MWh vanadium flow battery in Gujarat, supporting solar farms and reducing curtailment by 15 %, aligning with the nation’s 10 GW storage target by 2030.

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels
learn.intro: Bifacial panels capture sunlight on both front and rear sides, boosting energy yields without larger footprints. This technology helps meet rising demand for solar power while preserving land for agriculture and other uses.
learn.c1_text: Transparent backsheets or glass allow reflected and diffused light to hit the rear cells. In bright deserts, albedo can add 10‑20 % more energy—like a mirror that turns otherwise wasted light into extra electricity.
learn.c2_text: Bifacial modules are mounted on elevated racks or trackers to maximize rear‑side exposure. The gap between panels (5‑10 cm) lets ground‑reflected light reach the back, similar to spacing trees to let sunlight filter through.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2022 solar tender encouraged bifacial modules, leading to 12 GW installations across Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh, where sand‑ground albedo improves yields and reduces levelized cost of electricity by ~5 %.
learn.stat1: Global bifacial market share: 30 % of new utility PV (2023)
learn.stat4: LCOE reduction with bifacial: 5‑7 % vs monofacial

learn.title: Lithium‑Ion Battery Storage
learn.intro: Lithium‑ion batteries store large amounts of energy in compact units, enabling fast response for grid stability, renewable integration, and peak‑shaving. Their rapid adoption is reshaping how electricity is managed worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Lithium ions move from the positive cathode to the graphite anode during charging, and reverse during discharge. A 1 MWh battery pack holds roughly 3,600 kWh, comparable to powering 300 Indian homes for a day.
learn.c2_text: These batteries provide frequency regulation, load shifting, and backup power within milliseconds. Their power‑density (≈250 W/kg) lets utilities smooth solar spikes, much like a fast‑acting shock absorber for the grid.
learn.c3_text: The Indian government’s 2024 Storage Mission targets 20 GW of lithium‑ion capacity by 2030, with projects like a 300 MW/600 MWh system in Tamil Nadu supporting solar farms and reducing curtailment by 12 %.

learn.title: Building Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV)
learn.intro: BIPV weaves solar cells directly into building materials—facades, windows, roofs—turning structures into power generators. This approach cuts material costs, enhances aesthetics, and accelerates clean‑energy adoption in urban environments.
learn.c1_text: Photovoltaic glass replaces conventional windows, generating 5‑10 W per square foot while still transmitting light. A 100 m² BIPV curtain wall can produce ~8 kWh/day, similar to the daily consumption of a small office.
learn.c2_text: BIPV reduces separate mounting hardware, lowers roof load, and counts as part of the building envelope. It also qualifies for green‑building credits, offering developers up to 5 % cost savings on LEED projects.
learn.c3_text: Mumbai’s 25‑story “Solar Tower” uses BIPV glass on its façade, delivering 1.2 MW of rooftop‑equivalent power and cutting the building’s electricity bill by 30 % under the city’s net‑metering scheme.
